core_thesis: Ananda K. Coomaraswamy (1877–1947), born in Colombo to a Ceylon Tamil father (Sir Muthu Coomaraswamy) and an English mother, trained as a geologist at University College London and directed the Mineralogical Survey of Ceylon (from 1903) before turning to art. From 1917 until his death he was Keeper of Indian art at the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, where he assembled and interpreted a major collection and did much to establish the study of Indian art in the West; in his later work he became, with René Guénon, one of the founders of the perennialist ("Traditionalist") school. Coomaraswamy presents art as a disciplined intellectual and contemplative act, not as private self-expression or naturalistic copying. In *The Transformation of Nature in Art*, the artist's work begins from inward visualization: the form is known in the heart through concentration, then made outwardly as a symbolic body for an intelligible reality. Images are therefore not fetishes but supports for meditation, comparable to diagrams, and their truth depends on canonical form, symbolic fitness, and the worshipper's contemplative use rather than empirical likeness ([The Transformation of Nature in Art, ch. I](cite://coomaraswamy/thetransformationofnatureinartanandcoomaraswamy/chapter-i-the-theory-of-art-in-asia); [The Transformation of Nature in Art, ch. VII](cite://coomaraswamy/thetransformationofnatureinartanandcoomaraswamy/chapter-vii-origin-and-use-of-images-in-india)). In *The Dance of Siva*, the same metaphysics appears aesthetically: rasa is a supersensuous experience akin to spiritual realization, while Nataraja's dance images cosmic creation, preservation, destruction, veiling, and release as one rhythmic divine activity ([Dance of Siva, Theory of Beauty](cite://coomaraswamy/dli-ernet-234006/hindu-view-of-art-theory-of-beauty); [Dance of Siva](cite://coomaraswamy/dli-ernet-234006/the-dance-of-siva)).
